model_summary: A compact, gVAR-ready initial observation model capturing 6 health-anxiety–related criteria (C01–C06) as changeable cognitive, behavioral, and emotional processes, and 5 modifiable predictors (P01–P05) spanning CBT-behavioral activation, exposure/inhibitory learning, metacognitive monitoring, sleep regulation, and psychoeducation. Measurements rely on brief 1–9 Likert EMA items collected 6 times per day across fixed blocks with jitter to maximize within-person variance, plus a 1× daily diary diary entry for sleep context. The design emphasizes non-redundant constructs with nonbinary scaling, aims for moderate within-person variance, and preserves interpretability for subsequent gVAR modeling. Lacteal restrictions: not a diagnosis, no pharmacological guidance, and explicit safety notes where needed.
variable_selection_notes: Selected 6 criteria (C01–C06) directly reflect the complaint’s core processes: health-focused worry, catastrophic interpretation, compulsive internet checking, inability to inhibit checking, perceived loss of control, and acute panic when symptoms appear. To minimize redundancy, a single broad cognitive-behavioral/control mechanism is captured through 5 predictors (P01–P05) that map to the most informative levers from HyDe/LLM mappings: behavioral activation, exposure/inhibitory learning, metacognitive stopping, sleep regulation, and psychoeducation. Within-person variance is encouraged with 6 daily EMA prompts and a daily sleep diary entry. Potential biases include self-report bias in health worry and craving-like urges; plan includes z-scoring and within-person centering for gVAR readiness.

momentary_ema.notes: Two prompts per block with jitter to cover mid-morning, early afternoon, and early evening periods.

daily_diary.notes: Evening sleep diary to contextualize C06 panic responses.

passive_streams.notes: No passive streams included in this initial pass to maintain simplicity.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Initial set is designed for rapid estimation; variables show expected within-person variance with moderate missingness tolerance.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: 6 prompts/day across 14 days yields stable variance; daily sleep diary adds contextual depth.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Plan for missing prompts via automated reminders and imputation-ready handling during analysis.

safety_notes: Not a diagnosis. No medication guidance. If acute risk signs emerge, follow standard safety protocols and encourage professional help.

measurement.notes: Direct target of reducing avoidance and distress.

expected_effects.rationale: Higher activation should reduce worry frequency/intensity over hours to a day.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: No medication guidance; ensure tasks are safe and within daily routine.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility within app-based EMA; requires user engagement.

Less distress via habituation; perceived control improves.

Decreases panic onset through monitoring control.
Sleep quality linked to threat appraisal.
Lower arousal at symptom onset with good sleep.
Education enhances engagement with exposure strategies.

criteria.label: Catastrophic interpretation of minor bodily symptoms
criteria.query_text_used: Catastrophic interpretation of minor bodily symptoms | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, rate the tendency to interpret minor bodily sensations as life-threatening on a 1-9 scale. | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures immediacy of catastrophic interpretation at symptom onset.

mapping.rationale: Best match: C02 Catastrophic interpretation of minor bodily symptoms maps to somatic symptom disorder / catastrophizing_thoughts_about_symptoms (idx 27106). The variable label and EMA item target catastrophizing of minor bodily sensations as life-threatening, which aligns with catastrophizing_thoughts_about_symptoms. Mechanism: cognitive appraisal bias driving health anxiety and amplification of somatic symptoms; a cognitive-behavioral process consistent with the HyDe/LLM mappings and the behavioral-cognitive control lever family (e.g., psychoeducation/metacognitive monitoring). This is the most specific, operational leaf for the given variable among candidates.

source_variable.label: Catastrophic interpretation of minor bodily symptoms

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, rate the tendency to interpret minor bodily sensations as life-threatening on a 1-9 scale.
measurement.notes: Captures immediacy of catastrophic interpretation at symptom onset.

criteria.label: Compulsive online health-checking (duration)
criteria.query_text_used: Compulsive online health-checking (duration) | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, approximate the time spent searching health information (1–9 scale; 1 = very little, 9 = several hours). | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Proxy for checking duration in hours; abstracted to a 1–9 scale for variance and simplicity.

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Compulsive online health-checking (duration)' with EMA item: 'time spent searching health information (1–9 scale) since last prompt' maps most directly to cyberchondria; leaf 26995 corresponds to 'somatic symptom and related disorders / cyberchondria / compulsive_internet_health_searching'. The measurement is momentary, via smartphone app, using a 1–9 Likert scale to proxy duration of online health information searching—precisely the intended construct. Mechanistically, this reflects health-anxiety–driven compulsive checking (cyberchondria) consistent with C03 and the model’s emphasis on cognitive-behavioral/control levers influencing health worry and checking.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, approximate the time spent searching health information (1–9 scale; 1 = very little, 9 = several hours).
measurement.notes: Proxy for checking duration in hours; abstracted to a 1–9 scale for variance and simplicity.

criteria.label: Failure to inhibit checking despite recognizing irrationality
criteria.query_text_used: Failure to inhibit checking despite recognizing irrationality | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, rate how successful you were at stopping health-checking when you knew it was irrational (1–9). | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Assesses inhibitory control in real-time.

mapping.rationale: Variable C04 assesses real-time inhibitory control over health-checking when irrational. The most specific operational match among candidates is the leaf 'resists_obsessive_behaviors_when_possible' (idx 29060) within OCD-related subtypes. This leaf directly represents the capacity to resist or inhibit obsessive checking behaviors, aligning with the measurement item that asks how successful the person was at stopping health-checking when they knew it was irrational. EMA self-report via smartphone fits the intended design, and the construct maps to inhibitory control mechanisms implicit in health-anxiety criteria (C04).

source_variable.label: Failure to inhibit checking despite recognizing irrationality

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, rate how successful you were at stopping health-checking when you knew it was irrational (1–9).
measurement.notes: Assesses inhibitory control in real-time.

criteria.label: Subjective sense of loss of control and distress about it
criteria.query_text_used: Subjective sense of loss of control and distress about it | Operational item/signal: Right now, rate how out of control your health anxiety feels (1–9). | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Immediate distress proxy for perceived loss of control.

mapping.rationale: Best match: variable label is Subjective sense of loss of control and distress about it; measurement item asks for immediate rating of how out of control health anxiety feels (1–9). The most specific operational leaf is fear_of_loss_of_control_present under panic/anxiety contexts, capturing present loss-of-control experiences and associated distress, which aligns with the variable's cross-domain health anxiety focus. Other leaves describe broader worry, misinterpretation, or health-specific symptoms rather than a direct loss-of-control experience, making them less precise mappings.

source_variable.label: Subjective sense of loss of control and distress about it

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate how out of control your health anxiety feels (1–9).
measurement.notes: Immediate distress proxy for perceived loss of control.

criteria.label: Acute panic-level fear when a symptom appears
criteria.query_text_used: Acute panic-level fear when a symptom appears | Operational item/signal: Immediately after noticing a symptom, rate your fear/panic level (1–9). | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures abrupt arousal spikes driving urgent behavior.

mapping.rationale: Variable label: Acute panic-level fear after noticing a symptom maps to a sudden panic-like arousal in response to a cue. The best-matched leaf is 17303, which specifies panic_like_anxiety_spikes as a measurable construct within anxiety disorders (specific phobia path in the leaf, but the leaf provides the generic 'panic_like_anxiety_spikes' evidence). The measurement item (immediately after noticing a symptom, rate fear/panic level on a 1–9 scale via EMA) aligns with the momentary, nonbinary Likert EMA approach described in the pseudoprofile. This provides the most direct operational construct capturing abrupt panic responses to symptom cues among the candidates.

source_variable.label: Acute panic-level fear when a symptom appears

measurement.item_or_signal: Immediately after noticing a symptom, rate your fear/panic level (1–9).
measurement.notes: Captures abrupt arousal spikes driving urgent behavior.

predictors.label: CBT_Behavior_Change_Activation
predictors.query_text_used: CBT_Behavior_Change_Activation | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Engagement in planned coping/activation reduces cognitive load and distress. | Intervention examples: Scheduled activities, graded task exposure, activity scheduling | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, rate your engagement in planned coping/behavioral activation tasks (1–9). | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies > Behavior_Change_and_Activation

mapping.rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf is 4112: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / behavior_change_and_activation / planning_and_grading / activation_design / graded_task_assignment. Variable CBT_Behavior_Change_Activation explicitly maps to activation design and graded task assignment in CBT-based behavior activation. The EMA item asks to rate engagement in planned activation tasks since the last prompt, aligning with a graded_task_assignment approach (structured activation progression). This is the most specific operational construct among candidates, providing a clear mechanism for activation through graded tasks, which supports within-person variance tracking in gVAR-ready modeling.

source_variable.label: CBT_Behavior_Change_Activation
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Engagement in planned coping/activation reduces cognitive load and distress.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Scheduled activities, graded task exposure, activity scheduling

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, rate your engagement in planned coping/behavioral activation tasks (1–9).
measurement.notes: Direct target of reducing avoidance and distress.

expected_effects.rationale: Higher activation should reduce worry frequency/intensity over hours to a day.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: No medication guidance; ensure tasks are safe and within daily routine.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility within app-based EMA; requires user engagement.

predictors.label: CBT_Exposure_and_Inhibitory_Learning
predictors.query_text_used: CBT_Exposure_and_Inhibitory_Learning | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Structured exposure and learning to inhibit safety behaviors can reduce catastrophic interpretations and checking. | Intervention examples: Interoceptive exposure to mild symptoms; scheduled information-search reduction | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, rate to what extent you engaged in exposure to health fears or resisted checking (1–9). | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies > Exposure_and_Inhibitory_Learning

mapping.rationale: Variable P02 maps to CBT exposure and inhibitory learning; measurement asks to rate exposure to health fears or resistance to checking, which aligns with safety-behavior inhibition (competing_response_prevention) within safety_work/identify_and_remove; this leaf is the most specific operational construct directly capturing exposure/inhibitory learning to reduce checking.

source_variable.label: CBT_Exposure_and_Inhibitory_Learning
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Structured exposure and learning to inhibit safety behaviors can reduce catastrophic interpretations and checking.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Interoceptive exposure to mild symptoms; scheduled information-search reduction

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, rate to what extent you engaged in exposure to health fears or resisted checking (1–9).
measurement.notes: Targets habituation to feared cues.

expected_effects.rationale: Exposure reduces catastrophic threat interpretation and urge to check.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Safe, structured tasks; no exposure to actual danger.

feasibility.notes: Moderate burden; ensure brief exposure tasks with clear boundaries.

predictors.label: Metacognitive_Stop_Monitoring
predictors.query_text_used: Metacognitive_Stop_Monitoring | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Control of threat-monitoring and checking supports improved cognitive flexibility. | Intervention examples: Stop-threat monitoring, cognitive defusion, attention redirection | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, rate your success at stopping threat monitoring/checking (1–9). | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > cognitive_and_metacognitive_interventions > metacognitive_and_attention_based_interventions

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf maps directly to Metacognitive_Stop_Monitoring: path includes stop_threat_monitoring_and_checking and the variable name matches P03. The measurement item asks to rate success at stopping threat monitoring/checking, aligning with metacognitive stop-monitoring constructs. Rationale also notes that reducing threat monitoring supports cognitive flexibility; the EMA self-report measurement (6 prompts per day) aligns with within-person metacognitive monitoring targets.

source_variable.label: Metacognitive_Stop_Monitoring
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Control of threat-monitoring and checking supports improved cognitive flexibility.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Stop-threat monitoring, cognitive defusion, attention redirection

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, rate your success at stopping threat monitoring/checking (1–9).
measurement.notes: Anchors rapid cognitive shifts.

expected_effects.rationale: Reductions in threat monitoring should lower compulsive checking episodes.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Non-invasive cognitive strategy; no safety concerns.

feasibility.notes: Requires training on defusion/monitoring techniques; feasible with brief prompts.

predictors.label: Sleep_Routines_and_Practices
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep_Routines_and_Practices | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Sleep quality and regularity strongly modulate arousal and health anxiety reactivity. | Intervention examples: Regular bedtimes, limiting screen exposure before bed | Measurement: EMA_self_report | How would you rate last night's sleep quality on a 1–9 scale? (1 = very poor, 9 = excellent). | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration > Circadian_Entrainment

mapping.rationale: Variable Sleep_Routines_and_Practices maps to sleep-related regulation. The best-matching leaf is sleep_quality_rating (last night's sleep quality) captured as a daily self-report log (end_of_day), under self_assessment_and_logs daily_and_retrospective_logs. This directly operationalizes sleep quality as a daily proxy for sleep routines and circadian entrainment effects on arousal/health-anxiety reactivity. Mechanistically, sleep quality modulates arousal and health anxiety reactivity, aligning with the variable's stated mechanism. The pseudoprofile context supports a diary-based sleep context while not overfitting to it.

source_variable.label: Sleep_Routines_and_Practices
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Sleep quality and regularity strongly modulate arousal and health anxiety reactivity.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Regular bedtimes, limiting screen exposure before bed

measurement.item_or_signal: How would you rate last night's sleep quality on a 1–9 scale? (1 = very poor, 9 = excellent).
measurement.notes: Sleep context informs next-day reactivity.

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep reduces acute panic reactions to symptoms.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: No safety concerns; monitor extreme sleep disturbance.

feasibility.notes: Evening sleep rating aligns with daily diary; high feasibility.

predictors.label: Psychoeducation_Modules
predictors.query_text_used: Psychoeducation_Modules | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Knowledge and self-monitoring skills support worry management and safety-seeking reduction. | Intervention examples: Brief psychoeducation fragments; cognitive coping skills | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, rate your engagement with psychoeducation content (1–9). | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Foundations_and_Common_Factors > Psychoeducation_Modules

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf under PSYCHO foundations_and_common_factors / psychoeducation_modules, specifically psychoeducation_meta_worry_and_safety_seeking (metacognition_and_beliefs_about_thinking). This aligns with Psychoeducation_Modules as a predictor by representing engagement with psychoeducation content that targets worry management and safety-seeking, and is the most directly relevant and specific sub-node among the candidates for a module-level, EMA-based engagement measure.

source_variable.label: Psychoeducation_Modules
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Knowledge and self-monitoring skills support worry management and safety-seeking reduction.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief psychoeducation fragments; cognitive coping skills

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, rate your engagement with psychoeducation content (1–9).
measurement.notes: Assesses exposure to educational content and its impact on worry.

expected_effects.rationale: Increased psychoeducation reduces general health worry.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Content safe; monitor for information overload.

feasibility.notes: Short-form content minimizes burden; encourages engagement.
